Annotation: Iosue knowing that the person which appeared was an Angel, and not God, nor a man, neither adored him with godlie honour, for that had been idolatrie, nor with ciuil, for that perteineth to worldlie and temporal excellence, and is not competent to sacred things, especialy to immortal and glorious spirits; and therfore the honour he did to this Angel, was religious honour infinitly inferiour to diuine, and yet much greater then ciuil. Religious honour due to Angels.

The Angel did not only accept of the honour done vnto him, but also required more, shewing that the verie place was holie for his presence, being otherwise the common field of Iericho. Holie places. See Annot. Exo. 10.

It is proper to God to see the secret cogitations of mens harts, of himselfe and by his owne power. And glorified Saints know our cogitations by seeing God, in whom al things appeare, that perteine to their state, and for the profit of others. S. Aug. li. de cura pro mortuis, c. 15. S. Greg. li. 12. c. 13. Moral. Prophets being yet mortal, by inspirations doe also see secret cogitations. 1. Reg. 9. 9. Reg. 14. &c.

Before Christs Gospel was promulgate, neither al Articles of faith were so expresly taught, nor the external profession thereof so strictly commanded, as now they are in time of more grace, which giueth more ayde to mans weaknes; wherein also more perfection is required. And therfore our Sauiour exacteth of al to confesse him, and his Religion, before men: els he wil denie them before his Father. Mat. 10. Likewise in the place where this Nobleman dwelt, his presence in the temple, and seruice to the King, could not be accounted a reuolt from true religion, which was neuer professed there, nor be scandalous to anie man, being al Infidels: but in a christian countrie, where al beare the name of Christians, especially where men are at controuersie about the true Christian religion, al that frequent, or repaire to the same assemblies, for publike seruice of God, are reputed to be of the same religion; or els dissemblers. This example therfore doth in no sort warrant going to the heretical Church: but contrariwise admonisheth al to take resolution in our case (as Naaman did in his) of our Eliseus, or spiritual Superiour.

It is euident by this place, that Thare, and some other progenitours of Israel sometimes serued false Gods, from which they were reduced: but Abraham was euer preserued in true religion; and the whole familie of Thare was therefore persecuted in Chaldea, as S. Augustine sheweth li. 16. c. 13. de ciuit. Likewise Theodoret q. 18. in Iosue, and other both ancient and late writers teach the same.

In that no mention is made of mourning for Iosue, S. Hierome noteth a mysterie, and a special point of Christian doctrine: It seemeth to me (saith he Epist. de 42. Mans. mans. 33.) that in Marie prophecie is dead, in Moyses and Aaron, an end is put to the law and priesthood of the Iewes. For so much as they could neither passe into the land of promise, nor bring the belieuing people out of the wildernes of this world. And (Mans. 34.) Aaron (sayth he) was mourned (and so was Moyses) Iesus is not mourned; that is, in the law was descent into hel (called limbus); in the Ghospel is passage to Paradise.

Becaufe Iosue commanded the Priests to take the arke, and stand with it in Iordan, for that also (chap. 5.) he ministred the Sacrament of Circumcision; likewise (chap. 8.) blessed the people, and (chap. 14.) renewed Gods couenant with them, English Protestants inferre, that he was chief superiour in spiritual causes; and therfore lay Princes are supreme heads and gouerners of the Church immediately vnder God. But none of these actions, nor al put together doe proue their purpose. For notwithstanding he very lawfully did these, and other like things, yet he had a spiritual superiour in earth, which was Eleazar the high-Priest. For Moyses being extraordinarie superiour of al, both in spiritual and temporal causes, the ordinarie priesthood and spiritual supremacie was established in Aaron and his sonnes. And the temporal gouernement after Moyses was giuen to Iosue, succeeding to him (Num. 27.) not in al, but in part of his glorie; his whole honour being distributed between the high-Priest, and the temporal Prince, as learned Theodoret (q. 48. in Num.) noteth. Where it is manifest that Iosue was not set ouer Eleazar, but Eleazar ouer him.
Deceipts and stratagems are lawful in iust warre; but not falsehood, nor breach of promise. S. Aug. q. 10. in Iosue.
These fiue thousand were of the thirtie thousand, which were first sent. v. 3. The other 25000, ioyned with Iosues troup, and entered into the citie.
Al superiours may blesse their subiects, Princes their people, and parents their children.
The manner of consulting our Lord was by the High-Priest praying in the tabernacle. Exod. 29.
In manie places we see the word Saviour, and like titles giuen to men, as the seruants and officers of God, who is the proper and principal Sauiour of al. S. Aug. q. 18. in Iudic.
In these 40. yeares are included the eight yeares of their seruitude, v. 8. and so in the rest of this historie, otherwise the number of yeares agreeth not with the count. 3. Reg. 6. v. 1.
Aod hauing special inspiration from God to doe this fact (as S. Augustine noteth vpon these words q. 20. in Iudic.) is not to be imitated by priuat men. See Num. 25. v. 1.
Iahel the figure was blessed amongst women: much more the most Holie Virgin Mother of God is blessed aboue al women.

Whether Iephte did wel or no in sacrificing his daughter, hauing vowed to offer in sacrifice whosoeuer or whatsoeuer should first meet him returning with victorie, is a great and hard question, saith S. Augustine (q. 49. in lib. Iudic.) and not easily decided, the holie Scripture neither approuing nor reprouing his fact. Neuertheles by conference of other Scriptures and discourse of reason, he iudgeth it most probable that Iephte offended in vowing without special warrant from God, to sacrifice that which by the law was not sacrificable; yet sinned not in performing his vow, but rather pacified God therby. S. Ambrofe (li. 3. de Officijs c. 12.) supposeth assuredly that this Prince Iephte offended in vowing vnaduisedly. S. Hierome also (li. 1. aduers. Iouinian.) approueth their opinion that say: It was Gods ordinance Iephte should feele the errour of his vnaduised vow, by the death of his daughter. S. Chrysostome (ho. 14. ad pop. Antioch.) and S. Gregorie Nazianzen (orat. de Machabeis) teach the same.

Manie things doe iustifie Samfsons fact in killing himselfe with the Philistiims. First, it appeareth by the miracle, that God directly and extraordinarily concurred by restoring in that moment his admirable strength, that he could pul downe two such pillers. And conformably we may gather, that God inspired his mind to attempt this fact, and so he erred not, but obeyed God herein, as S. Augustine noteth li. 1. c. 21. and 26. de ciuit. Dei. Secondly, he was moued with zeale of Gods honour, hearing the Idolaters praise their false God Dagon. Thirdly, he had a good and pure intention to reuenge himselfe for Gods more glorie, praying to him for restauration of strength. Fourthly, he did not directly desire to kil himselfe, but to kil the Philistiims; though himselfe must also die with them. And in this act especially he was a figure of Christ, who chiefly by his death conquered his enemies.

This child being of the tribe of Leui, though not of Aarons stock, was lawfully vowed to the seruice of the tabernacle, by his parents, during his childhood, but comming to yeares of discretion he was at his owne election to continue or to depart. If he had been of anie other tribe, he must haue been redeemed.
Leaue off to praise idoles, as ye haue accustomed to doe.
The Church of Gentiles. S. Aug. li. 17. c. 4. ciuit.
Neither Dauid nor Salomon, much lesse anie other King, possessed or iudged the ends of the earth: but Christs inheritance reacheth to the ends of the earth. Psal. 2. v. 18.

Sinnes directly against God, and that hinder his seruice, are more hardly remitted: but none at al are irremissible before death, because during life euerie one may truly repent, if he wil: and to al true penitents God promisseth remission of sinnes. Ezech. 33.

This was fulfilled as in the figure in Samuel, not wholly, for priesthood stil remained in the line of Aaron, as appeareth in Achias, Abiathar, and Sadoc. ch. 14. 22. &c. 3. Reg. 8. but perfectly in Christ. S. Beda. qq. in 1. Reg. c. 2.
Rare things are called precious, and so the guift of prophecie is here termed, which was then granted to few.
Their confidence of helpe from God, by presence of the arke, was good and commendable, but their sinnes deserued to be punished.
This zeale of religion in Heli towards the arke, is a great signe that he erred not in faith, but that he did not sufficiently correct his sonnes.
So soone as Christs Ghospel or Testament came among the Gentiles, al false Gods and idolatrie fel downe. S. Beda. q. in 1. Reg. c. 5.

The arke being a holie thing, as Relikes are, was terrible to their false God, the diuel; so the Relikes of S. Babilas ouerthrew the false God Apollo, as S. Chrysostom testifieth at large, li. cont. Gentiles, &c. 5.
Obstinate sinners doe harden their owne harts, not God, but by suffering them so to doe. See Annot. Exod. 7.

For so much as God had chosen Israel a peculiar people to himselfe, and hitherto ruled the same by his Priests established among them, and by Iudges extraordinarily raised vp and sent by him, to deliuer them in their distresses, their demand now to haue a King, who (after the manner of other nations) should be their Lord, is interpreted, as in effect to reiect God: in that they disliked, and sought to change his forme of gouernment. And therfore this request of the people iustly displeased both Samuel and God himselfe.

Samuel here by Gods appointment, to diswade the people from their desire of a King, reciteth such things, as Kings abusing their power doe oftentimes practise, by reason of their high dignitie, but vniustly and vnlawfully; according to the doctrine of ancient Fathers. S. Cyprian calleth the exactions of Kings here recited, grieuous iniuries. S. Hierome, dura imperia and seruitutem. S. Gregorie proueth the same by contrarie examples. Neuertheles Kings haue great prerogatiues, besides and aboue, but neuer contrarie to the law. Furthermore in case Kings or other Princes commit excesses, and oppresse their subiects, yet are they not by and by to be deposed by the people, but must be tolerated with patience, til God by his souerereigne authoritie, left in his Church, dispose of them.
God wil punish the sinnes of the people, by suffering euil Princes to reigne. Iob. 34. v. 30.
One that by diuine inspiration foreseeth things to come.
Oppression of innocents crieth to heauen.

S. Gregorie here noteth, that such as are placed in height of gouernment are annointed with oyle, which signifieth mercie, light, and curing of others. But the little vessel foreshewed that Saul not perseuering in grace, should be deposed from his Kingdome. li. 4. c. 5. in 1. Reg. 10.
Samuel enioyneth obedience to Saul to trie his humilitie. S. Greg. li. 4. c. 5. in 1. Reg. 10.
God gaue him peculiar grace for executing the office of a King.
By and by also the guift of prophecie.
Their superiour.
By lot the people were assured that the election was of God. S. Greg. ibidem.

As the men of Iabes deluded their enemies by equiuocation, so speaking that they were otherwise vnderstood then they ment: so the seruants of God being tempted with concupiscence of gloutonie (signified by Naas) must deceiue their carnal appetite, by promising to satisfie the desire of the flesh, but in deed keep such temperance, as they may kil the concupiscence, and not be killed by it. S. Greg. li. 5. c. 1. in 1. Reg. 11.
He offended in offering sacrifice, being neither a Priest, nor extraordinarily allowed to doe that office, and for this and other faults was deposed.
Gods foresight of sinne, and preordination to punish it, taketh not away free wil, nor possibilitie of welldoing. S. Aug. li. 3. c. 4. de lib. arbir.
Ominous speaches are proued by this and some other examples to be sometimes of God, though sometimes this kind of obseruation is superstitious. As before is noted, Gen. 24.
Pray no more nor expect no longer; so they proceeded to battel without further warrant.
Amalec is strucken when the flesh is chastised by abstinence. S. Greg. li. 6. c. 1. in 1. Reg. 15.
Destroyed when the minde is restrained from vncleane cogitations. S. Greg. li. 6. c. 1. in 1. Reg. 15.
God is said improperly to repent, when he altereth the thing that he did before. S. Amb. li. de Noe c. 4.
Christ ouercomming the diuel receiued the Church to his spouse. S. Greg.
Saul knew not Dauid, being perhaps in a shepheards habite: though he had not long before serued and pleased him wel. ch. 16. v. 21.
This happened not immediately after the victorie against Goliath, but when Dauid had dwelt some time in Sauls house, and was very grateful to him, and to al the court and people.
When the praises of one import diminishing of an other, he that is proud, as Saul now was, is moued to enuie and malice, more and more as the vertues of the other and his praises doe increase.
Distinction of common and holie bread. Also an example of dispensation in case of necessitie.

Dauid most wisely in face of the King of Geth, by diuine inspiration, feigned himself to be a foole. The Holy Ghost sheweth that the seruants of God may doe such things not of feare but of diuine wisdom.

By this it appeareth that Dauid consulted God by the high Priest: and the high Priest for this purpose ioyned the priestly ornament called Ephod, to the Rationale, and so had reuelation from God. Exo. 28. Leuit. 8.
Conditionally: If thou tarie here.
A tender conscience had scruple of a small doubt which was no sinne: a large conscience sticketh at nothing.

Saul being annointed King by Gods appointment, could not lawfully be slaine by his subiects without like ordinance from God. For though Dauid was also already annointed, yet that was not to reigne presently, but when Saul should die, or otherwise be taken away.
Not a malicious mind, such as Saul bore vniustly against Dauid, but punishment is decreed against Nabal, for not only denying a reasonable request, but also for reproachful answer.
Dauid is resolute, and often repeateth, that it is not lawful for priuate subiects to kill their Prince, no although himselfe was annointed to succeed.
These countries were neither subiect to the Philistiims nor to the Israelites, and were also of those nations whom God had commanded to destroy, dwelling within the land of Chanaan. Deut. 25.

It is not defined nor certaine, whether the soule of Samuel appeared, or an euil spirit tooke his shape, and spake to Saul. S. Augustine (lib. 2. q. ad Simplician.) proposeth both opinions as probable. He sheweth that Samuels soule might appeare, either brought thither by the euil spirit, or that the spirit of the holy Prophet was not raised by force of the inchantment, but by Gods secrete ordinance. In another worke (de cura pro mortuis gerenda, c. 15.) he sayth expressly, Samuel the Prophet being dead, foretold future things to King Saul yet liuing. This last iudgement is confirmed: first, by the text literally affirming that Samuel appeared; secondly, this apparition came sooner then expected; thirdly, the Authour of Ecclesiasticus (ch. 46.) amongst the praises of Samuel sayth: He slept and certified the King, and shewed to him the end of his life; fourthly, the diuel could not naturally foretel such things; fiftly, most Fathers are of the same iudgement: S. Iustinus Martyr, S. Basil, S. Ambrose, S. Hieronym, Iosephus, and manie other writers.
He speaketh by amplification, to make his fact seeme more reasonable: whereas the time of Dauids abode with him was but foure months. ch. 27. v. 7.
It is not against Gods commandment, Deut. 4. and 12. to make new lawes, so they be conformable and not contrarie to Gods former lawes.
Exequies of Saul obserued with mourning, weeping and fasting.
The Philistiims were strong and cunning archers, therefore Dauid commanded that his subiects should learne and exercise the same manner of fight.
They anointed him againe in confirmation of their acknowledging Gods ordinance.
King Dauid now atchieued that which the tribe of Iuda could not in the time of Iosue.
Idols that haue eyes and can not see, feete and can not goe, shal not enter into the Church of Christ.

Either there was no probable danger, and so without cause Oza touched the arke, that he might seeme to be very careful; or els he vsed not so much reuerence as he ought to haue done. 1. de mir. Sac. Scrip. apud S. Aug. tom. 3.
To dance before the arke is to dance before our Lord.

The tabernacle made by Moyses was a goodlie thing, but being couered with skinnes and in manie respects insufficient for Gods seruice, Dauid desired to build a glorious Temple. But was not permitted to doe it for mysterie sake, to signifie that Christ the true Salomon should build his Church.
He that supposeth this great promise to be fulfilled in Salomon, erreth much.
S. Paul expoundeth this of Christ, Heb. 1. v. 5.
This can not be said of Christ, but of Solomon, and of any Christian.
The See Apostolike, and Priestly power in the Church of Christ, is this perpetual Kingdome. S. Epiphanius Heresit. 29.
Here and in manie other places the Hebrew word is of the plural number, Elohim, Gods, signifying more diuine Persons.
For more reproch Dauid compelling them with cords, as cattle are enuironed, cast them on the ground, and by lot killed some, and spared some aliue.

This Naas King of Ammon curteously entertained Dauids friends which escaped from the King of Moab, killing most of them that were commended to him, because Dauid had left his countrie, and was returned into Iuda.

Theodosius the Emperour pretending to be excused from punishment for his sinnes, because King Dauid also was an adulterer and a manslaver, S. Ambrose replied, saying: Thou that hast followed King Dauid erring, follow him repenting. After which admonition the Emperour most humbly did publike penance; inioyned him by the Bishop.

Now and then some of thy seed shall be violently slaine: so were slaine three of his owne sonnes, Amnon, chap. 13. Absalom chap. 15. Adonias, 3. Reg. 2. also Iorams sonnes saue one, 2. Par. 21. also Ochozias, Amasias, Iosias, 2. Par. 14. 25. 35. and the sonnes of Sedechias, himselfe hauing his eyes put out and so brought into Babylon. 4. Reg. 25.
King Dauid was here abused by false information: to which he ought not so easily to haue giuen credite. chap. 19. v. 14.

God suffered Semei, being of his owne freewil malicious, for punishment of Dauids sinnes, to curse him: but was not the authour of his malice, for so Semei had committed no fault therin, and then he could not lawfully haue been punished for it, as he was 3. Reg. 2.

The people doubting lest Absalom might be reconciled to his father, were not assured vnto him, til they saw such a crime committed as seemed to make reconciliation impossible. So al rebelles and vsurpers of others right, seeke by some enormious fact to make their adherents and followers sure vnto them: but God plagueth them in the end.
Bad counsell often falleth worst to the counseller.
Al his sonnes being dead, for he had once three sonnes and a daughter. chap. 14. v. 27.

Al the eleuen tribes are called by the name of Ioseph, being chiefe after Iuda. So Semei not of the proper tribe of Ioseph, but of Beniamin, pleading for pardon of his former fault, alleadgeth that he came first of the eleuen tribes, to submitte himselfe and serue the King.

King Dauid in this last prophecie plainly distinguisheth between the couenant and promise made to him touching his earthlie Kingdome and the Kingdome of Christ, who should be borne of his seed. In both which he foretelleth the reward of the good and punishment of the bad.
The furie of our Lord, that is Satan (a furious spirite, yet Gods creature) not our Lord himselfe, but by permission only. 1. Par. 21. 1. Satan arose against Israel, and moued Dauid.
Contrition and Confession.
Satisfaction.
For this conspiracie Abiathar was deposed. ch. 2. v. 27.

Salomon was not only a King but also a Prophet. Moreouer some secular Princes doe nominate spiritual superiours, and install them in their states, yet their iurisdiction is not depending on the Prince, but the Prince to be directed by them.
So heretikes not being able to proue that their synagogue is the true and permanent Church, would destroy the Catholike and so haue none at al.
These books are not extant.
In perfect people is neither noise of murmur in aduersitie, nor of boasting in prosperitie, but patience and humilitie with silence.
It is a cleer case, that al carued and grauen pictures, or images were not vnlawful, but were religiously made and set in the holie Temple, for the more honour of God.

Salomon knew wel Gods conditional promise, but perseured not in keeping his commandments, and therfore a great part of the Kingdome was taken from his children, yet the right of the Kingdome of Iuda remained to his seed euen to Christ our Sauiour.

A wonderful thing, that a Queene vpon fame of a mans wisdome traueled so farre to heare him speake, and to see his gouernment, but it was Gods inspiration to signifie by this figure, that the Church of Christ should be gathered of the Gentiles in al nations. Isai. 49.
From the time that Salomon fel to idolatrie, he was more impugned by three perpetual aduersaries, Adad, Razon, and Hieroboam; mystically signifying the flesh, the world, and the diuel.
This fact confirmed his words, that he spake seriously and fained not.
This man of Bethel was indeed a Prophet of God, but in this lied wickedly, and so deceiuing the other Prophet, made him to breake Gods commandment, for which he was slaine.

Ieroboam did not wittingly and of purpose set vp false Gods, to the end he might prouoke God to anger; for his intention only was to keep the people from going to Ierusalem, lest by that occasion they should returne to Roboam.

Those altars which Salomon had made for his wiues that were idolaters Asa destroyed not, but al which Roboam and Abias had made, or suffered to be made for their owne people, he pulled downe. Iosias afterward destroyed also those which Salomon had made.

To this question (saith S. Augustin) the Prophet answered in spirite: No. For God killed not this childe to afflict so good a mother, but to confirme her in true religion and comfort her by raising him from death. So Lazarus died not to remaine dead, but to be raised to life, for Gods more glorie. S. Aug. li. 2. q. ad Simplicia.
Such zelous expostulation is necessarie to al Neutrals in religion who are neither hot nor cold, but luke warme, such as Angels detest. Apoc. 3.

Elias knew of no other at that instant in the tenne tribes, that were not partakers in idolatrie; but there were in deed seuen thousand (v. 18.) that bowed not their knees to Baal. And the whole Kingdom of Iuda obserued true religion.
Manie victories and other benefits were bestowed vpon Achab, to make him know God: but contemning them al remained in his impietie, and finally was slaine.

If no subiect were Lord of anie land, but only at the Kings pleasure, and Kings were proper Lords of al the lands in their Kingdomes, then Achab might haue taken Naboths vineyard. Neither was it a vaine scruple in Naboth, to conserwe his ancestors inheritance. For both his deniall is here iustified, and Achabs extortion condemned. S. Ambrose li. 2. offic. c. 9.
To auoid horrour of blasphemie holie Scripture often vseth the terme blessing for cursing.
The godlie King Iosaphat iustly suspecting the schismatical false Prophets, aduised the other King to consult a true Prophet of God.
False Prophets imitate true Prophets in some exterior things, to make their Prophecies seeme more authentical. Such hornes appeared in a propheticall vision to Zacharie the Prophet, Zach. 1. v. 18.

God sent his law by his seruant, and it auailed not to mankind dead in sinnes. But he comming and accommodating himselfe to our infirmitie, participating our death, we are quickened. So S. Augustine mystically applieth this figuratiue miracle to Christ and his Church. li. 12. c. 35. cont. Faust. Manich.
In respect of Gods special electing and sanctifying the land of Chanaan, by his true religion, Naaman rightly esteemed that earth fitter for an altar, then the earth of his owne countrie.

Giezi prefigured Iudas the false Apostle of Christ, and al those that buy or sel spiritual things for money, who by their auarice lose Gods grace, and gaine infamie in this world, and eternal damnation in the next. S. Aug. ser. 108. de tempore.
Discourse of mans reason can not reach to the power of God, who can doe al that he wil, and wil doe al that he saith: therefore the incredulous are iustly punished. v. 10.
This was true in some sense, sicknes ending when death came.
Moral good workes done in state of mortal sinne are not meriting eternal life, but are often temporally rewarded.
Ambition cause of much crueltie.
That is, their estimarie oblation, for ech particular person. Exod. 30.
Mystically signified to the Prophet that so often as the King should strike the earth, so often he should get victories against the Syrians. But he knew not how often he should strike on the earth.

This image was a type or figure not only recounting a miraculous health, but also long after was specially kept in memorie of the benefite: but when the people of Israel gaue it honour which is proper to God only, good Ezechias broke it. And so were those images no idols in themselues, but when there were who gaue them honour or seruice, called idolatrie. And so in the Catholike Church when any holy Relicke or Image is abused, it is taken away, or the error otherwise corrected.
God protected and preserued true religion, though the wicked practised abominations. Manasses in captiuitie became vertuous and recouered his Kingdome. 2. Par. c. 33.
Because they had offered sacrifice to false Gods and in vnlawful places, they were suspended from offering any more sacrifice at al.

Certaine false Prophets perswaded the King and the people not to beleeue the Prophets, who forewarned them of these calamities, because they said the one contradicted the other. Ieremie said (ch. 32.) Sedecias should see the eyes of Nabuchodonosor, and would be led into Babylon; Ezechiel said (ch. 12.) he should not see Babylon. Both were true: for he was caried thither after his eies were put out.
Adam had two other sonnes before Seth, but Cains race was extinguished by the flood, and Abels had no children.
See annotations Gen. 49. v. 4.
That is, confirmed and preserue thy seed, and familie, as we see it performed euen to the B. Virgin Marie and Christ of the house of Dauid. Mat. 1. Luc. 1. 3.
By Gods permission Dauid was tempted and ouercome. 2. Reg. 24.
King Dauid was not without faith, nor hope, and yet was contaminate with iniquitie, from which he prayed to be deliuered.
Either his first sonne was dead, or was not fitte to be chiefe ouer the rest.
Things are sanctified by designation to holy vse.
As wel the Temple as the Altars, and other appertainances were made after the forme of Moyses Tabernacle, but greater in quantitie, of more precious matter, and more excellent workmanship.
In this chapter and oft els where it is euident, that places dedicated to Gods seruice are more acceptable to him, then other places.
They are called Kings of God, which reigne by his grace and according to his wil.
Cooperation with Gods grace meriteth increase of grace.
He destroyed the places where sacrifice was offered to idols, but tolerated other places where the people offered to God our Lord without the temple, not being able to reduce al to perfection.
They are wilfully blind that wil not see difference between images of Baal and of Christ or of his Saints.
Obduration of hart for former sinne.
Wicked policie auaileth nothing, but hurteth much.
Being penitent in hart for their sinnes, Gods dispensation might be supposed for legal purification in case of necessitie, which otherwise was strictly commanded.
Voluntarie workes of supererogation, more then was commanded.
More danger of pride in prosperitie, then in aduersitie.
Giuen by the hand of Moyses.
It is a benediction to be taken out of this world, before general miserie come vpon the people.
It is like that Esdras added this conclusion when he restored the holie Scriptures that were lost, for he beginneth his owne booke with the same wordes.

According to the historie Ioatham Gedeons yongest sonne, by a parable iustly expostulateth the iniurie done by the Sichemites to his fathers house, in preferring a base bond-womans sonne, and cruelly murdering the rest of his sonnes: who with much trauel, and manie dangers of his owne life, had deliuered them from seruitude. As in the spiritual sense, which (as the ancient fathers note) is chiefly intended, Idolaters and Heretikes are reproued, who rather accept of vniust vsurpers, that wil serue their licentious appetites, and mantaine vice and wickednes, then to be ruled by iust and lawful men Superiours, appointed by Gods ordinance, indued with grace of the Holie Ghost (signified by the oliue tree); such as bring forth wholesome sweet vertues signified by the figge tree; and are replenished with admirable fortitude (signified by the vine tree); and in their places set vp base, ambitious, cruel, and crabbed spirits, signified by the bramble, or brier. Thus Nemrod, Abimelech, Mahomet, and innumerable other tyrants haue been aduanced, & especially Antichrist shal be exolled aboue al that is called God, or is worshipped, and shal most cruelly persecute al Catholikes, that wil not conforme themselues to his proceedings. But in fine (as here is prefigured in Abimelech) so shal rise against this bramble Antichrist, and shal deuour him and al his together. S. Beda. qq. in lib. Iudic. c. 6.
In the old testament marriage was ordinarily preferred before single life, but in the new it is better to keepe virginitie. 1. Cor. 7. v. 36.

Al ancient fathers and Catholike writers say, this was an Angel, which appeared in the forme of a man, and it is plaine by the text. Yet some Protestants wil haue this person to be Christ, the eternal word of God, Who afterwards became man. And neuertheles where by and by (v. 16.) he admonisheth Manue to offer sacrifice to God, they note that he sought not his owne honour but Gods, whose messenger he was: either plainely contradicting themselues, or els teaching Arrianisme, as though the Sonne of God were not God; or inferiour to God the Father.
Liberally gaue such things into their hands.
This enumeration of the Israelites, which ascended into Jerusalem, signifieth the Elect which ascend from the militant Church to the triumphant.
Esdras came with the first from Babylon (2. Esd. 1.) but returned thither, and now ascended the second time to Jerusalem.
Malachias the Prophet complaineth also of this fault, c. 2. v. 11. threatening Gods punishment both to superiours and subiects for not correcting it.

In respect of their great iniquities Esdras presumeth not to aske the conseruation of the whole people, but some relikes or smallest part, as it were a little post, or a naile of a whole house towards the reedifying thereof.

Nehemias being sent from a King; by his name which signifieth comfort from our Lord; by his building againe the wals of Jerusalem; prefigured our Sauiour, who was sent from God the Father, himselfe being the comforter of mankind, and the sender of another comforter the Holy Ghost, to remaine with his Church.

When heretikes and other aduersaries of the Church finde themselues not able to suppresse Catholikes, they offer conditions of peace and libertie to al: So when Protestants began, and where they are weake, they would haue none persecuted for anie opinions in religion; but where they are strong they hardly grant toleration to Catholikes.
Athersatha priuiledged, by reason of his fauour with King Artaxerxes.
In al leagues and couenances of peace those articles are specially mentioned wherin breach hath been made in former times.
As others gaue tithes to the Leuites: so they gaue to Priests. Num. 18. v. 21. 28.
The Church vseth this salutation in the holie sacrifice and other diuine office.
Booz doubted not but reward was due to good workes.
Yea a ful reward, answerable to Ruths pietie: Which must be spiritual and eternal.
Booz calleth his kinsman brother, as Abraham called Lot his brother Gen. 13. being his Nephew.
See Deut. 25. noting here withal, that the penaltie was lesse, when an other kinsman vndertaking the mariage, the woman was preuented from complayning before the iudge.

Here appeareth the final cause of writing this historie, to shew the Genealogie of King Dauid from Iudas the Patriarch, of whom Christ should descend, so prophecied, Gen. 49. and shewed to be performed, Mat. 1.
This Patriarch first called Iacob signifying supplanter, was afterward called Israel, that is, Seeing, or valient with God. Gen. 35.
Either this man had two names, or there is errour in the last letter, here, or Iosue 7.
Otherwise called Calubi. v. 9.
By fonnes, as often elsewhere, are vnderstood nephewes, & other offspring.
Valient men; by whose help Ioab got victories and triumphant crewnes.
S. Matthe omitteth this Ioakim, and counteth Iechonias as the sonne of Iosias. 4. Reg. 24. v. 6. & 25. v. 27. 5. Hierem. li. 1. in Masth.
Semeia with his fiue fonnes are counted six; though Sedecias, though onely was his proper sonne, the other his nephewes. See annotations, ch. 1. num. 3.

In ioyning a vow to his prayer he imitated holie Iacob. Gen. 18. And they both desired temporal things for the better seruing of God, & aduancing his glorie; especially that they might be afflicted with grace not to yeald to tentations, nor sinne of malice.
Chief Lord of the valley; where the artificers dwelt that made the Temple.

In Sadoc. (2. Reg. 2.) the high Priesthood was reduced to the line of Eleazar which by Gods ordinance was translated to Ithamars line: but stil continued in the line of Aaron. The rest of Sadochs line by Achimaas &c. to Iosedech in the captiuitie in Babylon. Nicephorus counteth some others amen this, but in Greeke and Latin, as there receiued, ted. And so in the rest is much difference: but al may be reconciled by such rules as are noted.
Gen: 46. this third fonne is called Asbel.
Ishot, Virum decoram, that is, A comelic, or personable, or goodlie man. So we leaue the hebrew place because S. Ierom, and the whole Church doth so in the latin text, which we translate.
These are the fonnes of Ahod, Princes of their kinreds that dwelt in Gabaa, which were transported into Manahath.
The genealogies of al Israel being hitherto recited before their captiuitie, others are now added which first returned to Ierusalem after their release.

Saul therfore died for his iniquities, tor that he (a) transgressed the commandement of our Lord which he had commanded, and kept it not: yea and besides also consulted the Pythonesse, 14. and trusted not in our Lord: for the which he slew him, and transferred his Kingdome to Dauid the fonne of Isai.
He offered sacrifice on an Altar without warrant. 1. Reg. 11. And destroyed not the Amalacites, as he was commanded.
The third part. Kings Dauids reigne and his speciall actes.
Two stout men of Moab, as if they had been lions. 2. Reg. 23. v. 10.
An armie were (a) the armie of God like the great number of Angels, which to vs are innumerable. Iob. 25.
(a) Obededom was a Leuite, (c.15.v.18.) and therfore more meete to keepe the arke: (b) Called a Getheite because he had dwelt in the towne of Geth.
And the name of Dauid was bruited in al countries, and our Lord gaue the dread of him ouer al nations.
Another linen garment vsed by Prophets, such as Samuel did weare being a child. 1. Reg. 2.

Not only King Dauid being a holie Prophet, but any other Superiour might blesse his subiects, for Superiours power is proued by Gods institution, rather then by factes, either of good men, which doe manie things by way of dispensation, or of euil vsurping whithout warrant, that to them perteyneth not. For it is cleere that God instituted supreme spirituall power in the high Priest, Deut. 17. And al Kings and temporal Princes are to receiue the law at the Priests hand, ibidem. v.
Psal. 105.
That which is dedicated to sacred vse, is consecrated to God.
Salomon signifieth Peaceable, and manie other things prefigured Christ, who amongst other names is called Prince of peace. Isaiah 9.

Aarons fonnes being consecrated Priests according to Gods ordinance, Leuit. 8. the Leuites to doe other offices about the tabernacle, Num. 3. & 4. King Dauid (with Sadoc the high Priest, & other chief men, 1.14.v.6.) disposed them by lots which should serue by courtes, to sing & play on instruments, as wel in the tabernacle now resting in Ierusalem, v. 26. as in the Temple when it should be built.

They were called Princes (a) of the Sanctuarie, and Princes (b) of God, as wel of the children of Eleazar, as of the children of Ithamar. 6. And Semeias wrote them, the Scribe a Leuite, before the King and Princes, and Sadoc the Priest, and Ahimelech the fonne of Abiathar, the Princes also of the Priestlie and Leuitical families: one house, which was ouer the rest, Eleazars, and an other house which had the rest vnder it, Ithamars.
(a) Otherwise called Ethan, ch. 9.v.44.6.15.
(b) They played on instruments, Psalmes, and Canticles, made by Prophets.
King Dauid hauing heretofore offended in commanding to number the whole people, in commanding to number the whole people.
So Iacob prophecied that the royal scepter should come to the tribe of Iuda, & therin remaining til Christ. Gen. 49.
It is here euident, that Salomon was sometime the true seruant and childe of God can neuer become a wicked man.
That which he had already vowed & prepared he counted not his owne, and now giueth more to the building & furnishing of the Temple.
As Abraham, Isaac, Iacob & others were strangers in respect of Chanaan: so both they, and also Dauid, Salomon, and al men in this world, are pilgrimes in respect of heauen. Heb. 13.
Albeit they did the same exteriour act of honour to God and the King: yet in their mind and intention they gaue diuine honour to God, and ciuil to the King.
Either Nathan and Gad write the later part of the first book of Kings, or els their books are not now extant.
(a) It is the manner of holie Scripture to expresse things exceeding vulgar capacitie by the figure Hyperbole.

(b) It is probable that this man wife and most skilful, Hiram my father, the fonne of a Tyrian, who knowe ble that this man had instructed the King of Tyre in true religion of one God, whom he confesseth, v. 11. 12. and that therfore the King called his father.
(a) This mount Moria, signifying vision, was so named by Abraham, who was there readie to sacrifice his fonne Isaac. Gen. 11.
(b) And Dauid, by the appointment of an Angel, offered there sacrifice. 2. Reg. 24. 1. Par. 21.
(a) Another Arke was not made, because that was most holie and most excellent, which Moyfes made. Likewise other holie things of the tabernacle were brought into the Temple with great solemnitie.

(a) At the Temple was the special place of pray er, chosen by God, v. of the court before the temple of our Lord: for he had offered there the ta fo special holocausts, & the fatte of the pacifikes: because the brasen altar, which he had made, could not susteyne the holocausts and the sacrifices. and the fatte. 8. Salomon therfore made a solemnitie at that time feuen daies, and al Ifrael with him, an assemblie verie great, from the entrance of Emath to the Torrent of Egypt. And he made in the eight day an assemblie, because he had dedicated the altar feuen daires, and had celebrated the solemnitie feuen daies.

(b) Kneeling, (c) lifting vp hands, stretching forth armes, and the like external gestures doe much helpe internal attention in prayer, & also stirre vp others to godlie imitation: and therfore hath bee much practised by deuout persons both in the old and new Testament.
(d) In this chapter & ofte els where it is euident, that places dedicated to Gods seruice are more acceptable to him, then other places.
(c) Salomon was yet fo farre from communicating with Infidels, that he suffered not his wife an infidel to be present where the Arke of God had been placed. 1. Par. 15.
The second Diuision of the Kingdomes. Dauids issue reigning only in two tribes, which also are called true vnto Babylon.
(a) A worthie example of temporal damage rather then to conforme themselues to false religion.
(e) Faith is the foundation of al good works. Heb. 11. but not his wife, an infidel to be present where the Arke of God had been placed.
(a) A firme & perpetual couenant. Num. 8. v. 19.
(a) It perteyned properly to the King to destroy the exteriour practise of idolatrie, but to the Priefts & Prophees to informe the interiour mind & consciences of euerie one.

(a) Iust punishment with paine of his feet, for iniuriously putting Gods Prophet in fetters v. 10. So God punished him temporally, for that and other passionate sinnes: & he died in good state, for his hart was perfect al his dayes (c. 17.) that is, most part of his life, especially in his last dayes.
(a) Good works approued by new benefites from God, as a reward therof, doe giue more hope & confidence to proceede from vertue to vertue. S. Th. l. di regimme Regum.
(a) For this he was iustly reprehended, but his simple intention easily pardoned. li. 1. and his sinne, when he was in great danger, and repenting was pardoned. li. 2. c. 8. cons. epist. Parmen.
(b) See Annotation 3. Reg. 21. v. 15.

(a) To the impious man thou giuest ayde, and to them that hate our Lord thou art ioyned in freindship, and therfore thou didst deferue indeed the wrath of our Lord: 3. but good workes are found in thee, for that thou hast taken away the groues out of the land of Iuda, and hast prepared thy hart to feeke our Lord the God of thy fathers.
(b) A most plaine direction of spiritual & temporal authoritie and offices, not instituted by Iosaphat, nor any other King, but by God himselfe. Deut. 17. Num. 27.
(a) Elias was assumed from ordinarie conuersation with mortal men the eighteenth yeare of King Losaphat, 4. Reg. 2. 3. who reigned twentie fiue yeares, and his fonne Ioram reigned eight yeares.
(a) To wit when he began to reigne alone: for he reigned together with his father at the age of 22. 4. Reg. 8. v. 6. And after his fathers death but one yeare. See 4. Reg. 8. v.
(b) Humane hope failed, but Gods prouidence vsed meanes to conserue some of Dauids issue to fit in his throne. yea to continue the succession of Christ.
(a) By the law money payed yearly halfe a ficle, towards the repayring of the tabernacle, and so afterwards of the temple. Ex. 30.
(c) They are wilfully blind that wil not fee difference betweene images of Baal & of Christ or of his Saints.
(b) He that killed his spiritual father was slaine by his owne seruants.
(a) So long as this King obserued the ordinance of God to be directed by the high Priest Num. 27. v. 11. he prospered in his affaires.

(b) For vsurping spiritual authority which pertayned not to him, the high Priest with his assistantes opposed the King: and God confirmed their sentence by striking the same King with leprosie. And so he was not only cast out of the temple, but also out of his Kingdome, and common conuersation with other men, and forced to dwel in a separate house without the citie according to the law. Leuit. 13. v. 46.
(c) Neither could he be buried in the proper sepulchres of the Kings.
(a) Being penitent in hart for their sinnes, Gods dispensation might be supposed for legal purification in case of necessitie, which otherwise was strictly commanded. Leuit. 27. &c.
(b) Voluntarie workes of supererogation, more then was commanded.
(a) Besides sacrifices and pacifikes, there is also confession of Gods excellencie and goodnes.
(a) A pregnant example of the effect of hasty repentance.
(b) This prayer is not extant in the Hebrew but in Greeke and Latin, as yet neither receiued for canonical by the Church, nor refused.
(a) This Phase or Pasch made by Iosias is famous, partly for that this feast had been omitted for manie yeares, but specially for the great and extraordinarie solemnitie made at this time.
(b) Iosias thought that the King of Egypt intended to inuade his Kingdome. And it was Gods wil he should be slaine, and not the euils that should happen to the people.
(c) Solemne exequies with lamentations and musike.
(a) Notwithstanding the terrour of infidels, Gods feruants tooke courage to offer sacrifice.

(b) By the ordinance of Dauid, 1. Par. 15. By their weeping they testified that the new temple was not so excellent as the former. And therfore Aggeus prophecie (c. 2.) can not be vnderstood of this temple, but of the Church of Christ. S. Aug. li. 18. ciui.c.45.
(a) Schismatikes and Heretikes may not be admitted to communicate in sacrifice with Catholikes.
(a) This great number which by Esdras perswasion came from Babylon, signified the great fruit of soules conuerted from sinne by the exhortation of holy preachers. S. Beda li. 2. in Esdr. c. 10.

(b) It sufficeth not to part from Babylon (that is, from sinne) but we must also doe workes of satisfaction, and therfore Esdras here proclaimed an extraordinarie fast to those that were come from captiuitie.

(a) Esdras being extraordinarily sent by God to correct the people, spareth to the high Priefts fonne, by his authoritie calleth the people together, & so proceedeth to make reformation. So S. Paul conferred with other Apostles. Gal. 2.
(b) Their sinne was punished by ouer-much rayne, v. 13. And so affliction gaue the more feeling of their faults.
(a) Infidels and Heretikes are grieued, that others endeauour to repayre the ruines of the Church in any countrie.
(b) Gods hand was cleerly shewed in the effect of obtaining the Kings fauourable letters.
(a) Finishing the gate they dedicated it to Gods seruice, being for defence of his holy citie: & so sanctified it.

(a) It was Gods prouidence that the enemies mocking at the reparation of Ierusalem did not furiously resist, til the worke was performed: so sometimes heretikes scoffe at the endeauours of poore Priefts labouring to restore Catholike religion: but whether they scoffe or rage, Gods holy worke proceedeth and prospereth.
(a) S. Beda in his spirituall sense lamenteth that some Superiours neglected to feed their flock, either temporally or temporally, and yet exacted temporall reuenewes, and oppressed poore people.
(b) A right propertie of a true pastour, to doe that good which he preacheth to others. S. Beda ibidem.
(c) A good conscience hath great confidence in God, and iustly hopeth for reward.

(a) Three speciall defences of a citie, are the strength of wals, shutting and opening the gates in due time, & diligent watchmen: so to the custodie of faithfull soules three things are necessarie: the grace of God, due regard of the outward fences, and continuall watch against our inuisible enemies.
(a) True repentance requireth workes of penance, & especially the remouing of occasions of sinne: as separation from euil companie, abandoning of euil cogitations, and of much wordly pleasure.
(b) Changing of names importeth some beneficiall mysterie. Gen. 17.
(c) Free wil in sinners.

(a) Because Ierusalem was most impugned by enemies fewe were willing to dwel there, but yet many valiant men of the tribes of Iuda, Beniamin, and Leui, offered themselues: of other tribes the tenth part were chosen by lottes. Wherby is gathered that many of the ten tribes returned also into Chanaan, though the holie Scripture doth not expresly record what became of them.

(a) This Tobias was an Ammonite & a persecuter, c. 14. to whom Eliasib being a-kin (by reason of vnlawful mariages) ioyned in felowship with him wicked lucre: which therfore Nehemias corrected, perfiguring therin our Sauiours zele, who threw out the buyers and sellers out of the temple.
(a) A iust man that hath merited by good workes may pray with great confidence for reward.
